content.subtitle: Overview
items: In certain grammatical contexts, the past simple and past continuous tenses are employed not to describe past events, but to refer to present, future, or general circumstances.
Similarly, the past perfect simple and past perfect continuous can be used to describe a hypothetical scenario in the past — one that never actually took place.

content.subtitle: Watch out!
content.description: In all the structures discussed below that involve the past simple or past continuous, when the verb to be follows I, he, she, or it, both was and were are grammatically acceptable.
examples: ✓ I wish I **was** rich.
✓ I wish I **were** rich.

content.subtitle: Note
content.description: While was and were are both used naturally in informal contexts, were is often preferred in formal written styles, and appears exclusively in the fixed expression If I were you …

content.subtitle: For hypothetical and unlikely current, future or general conditions
examples: She wouldn't accept a promotion unless she **was** completely clear about the new responsibilities.
If you **were travelling** to Japan, would you exchange currency before leaving or on arrival?

content.subtitle: For hypothetical past conditions
examples: If I **had known**, I would have brought extra cash with me.
If I **had been managing** the project, I would have requested additional funding immediately!

content.subtitle: To consider hypothetical or unlikely current, future or general situations
examples: Supposing you were **offered** a scholarship worth fifty thousand euros, what would you do with it?
What if you were **walking** through the city and you stumbled upon a famous painting? Would you report it?

content.subtitle: To consider hypothetical or unlikely past situations
examples: Suppose you had inherited a fortune last year. What would you have invested in?
Imagine you'd been living abroad for the past decade. What kind of career would you have built?

content.subtitle: Watch out!
content.description: A present tense may also follow these introductory words and phrases, replacing the past simple or past continuous. When this is the case, the situation is presented as genuinely likely to occur.
examples: Supposing you become a millionaire, how will it change your life? (more likely)
Supposing you became a millionaire, how would it change your life? (less likely)

content.subtitle: For current, future or general untrue, hypothetical comparisons
examples: He speaks as if/though he was an expert on the subject.
Laura dresses as if/though she were earning a fortune every month.

content.subtitle: For past untrue, hypothetical comparisons
examples: Marcus looked as if/though someone had just handed him some terrible news.
It's almost as if/though they'd been training for this moment their entire lives.

content.subtitle: Watch out!
items: If the main verb appears in the present or present perfect tense, a past tense follows as if/though only when the comparison is clearly untrue. ✓ She behaves as if she were really wealthy. (= In reality, she is not.)

When the main verb is in the present or present perfect tense, and the comparison is a real possibility, we keep the following verb in a present or present perfect tense. ✓ She looks as if she's really wealthy. (= There is a genuine chance that she is.)

When the main verb is in a past tense, a past tense is used after as if/though regardless of whether the comparison is real or imagined. ✓ She looked as if she was really wealthy, though I knew she wasn't — so perhaps she was putting it on.

content.subtitle: To make questions and requests more polite
examples: What size did you want to try on, madam?
I was wondering whether you might be able to point me in the right direction.

content.subtitle: To suggest that something should be done now or in the immediate future
examples: It's (high/about) time I looked for a better-paid position.
It's (high/about) time we were heading off.

content.subtitle: Notes
items: When used with it's (high/about) time, the past simple and the past continuous are generally interchangeable in meaning. ✓ It's time I went home. ✓ It's time I was going home.
A full infinitive is also possible after it's time, although it cannot be used after the emphatic forms it's high time or it's about time. ✓ It's time to think about updating your CV.

content.subtitle: For current, general or future preference
examples: Anna would rather/sooner we rented a flat than committed to a mortgage straight away.
Would you rather/sooner I arranged the catering or handled the invitations?

content.subtitle: For past preference
examples: We'd rather/sooner you hadn't agreed to that arrangement without consulting us first.

content.subtitle: Watch out!
content.description: The structure would rather/sooner followed by a past simple or past continuous form is reserved for expressing preferences about another person's actions. If there is no change of subject, the bare infinitive follows would rather/sooner, or a full infinitive follows would prefer.
examples: ✓ She'd rather not take out a loan right now.
✓ She'd prefer not to take out a loan right now.

content.subtitle: Wishes about now, the future or generally
examples: Do you wish you **owned** a larger flat?
If only I **was earning** a decent wage.

content.subtitle: Wishes about the past
examples: If only I'd **applied** for that position when I had the chance.
Felix wished he'd **been keeping** a closer eye on his spending.

content.subtitle: Note
content.description: Even when wish itself is used in the past simple tense, it is still followed by a past simple or past continuous form to express current, future, or general wishes.
examples: ✓ Nina quietly **wished** that she **had** a bigger office.
✓ I sat there and **wished I was earning** a more reasonable salary.

content.subtitle: Other structures with wish / if only
items: To criticise other people or wish for a situation to be different → Wish / If only + would
I wish they **would** consider giving me a promotion.
Wishes about ability or permission → Wish / If only + could
If only I **could** find a role that suits my skills and pays well.
Wishes about past ability or permission → Wish / If only + could + perfect infinitive
I wish I **could have secured** a fixed-rate mortgage at the time.
To express desires in a very formal way → Wish + full infinitive
I wish to **lodge** a formal complaint.
To wish someone lucky/happiness/success/etc → Wish + noun
I wish you every **success** in your new venture.

content.subtitle: Important Notes
items: It is generally not acceptable to use **would** when the subject of wish and the subject of would refer to the same person. ✗ Pete wishes he would earn more.
Wish is not used to talk about genuine expectations or desires concerning a likely future outcome. ✓ Pete wishes he earned / could earn more.
In such cases, hope is the appropriate alternative. ✗ I wish the transfer goes through tomorrow. ✓ I hope the transfer goes through tomorrow.

sections.title: A: The Paper Trail
sections.instructions: Write the verbs in brackets in the correct form in each gap.

items.sentence: There is an intriguing historical puzzle that asks: 'If the ancient Chinese (1) ...... **(have)** all the information-sharing technology available to us today, would the exchange of ideas across the ancient world have happened more rapidly?'
(2) ...... **(we / be)** to consider this logically, we would find it nearly impossible to arrive at a satisfying conclusion.

Nevertheless, approaching it with creativity reveals a striking insight. (3) ...... **(the ancient Chinese / be)** as connected as the modern world, they would still have faced one remarkable bottleneck.

What bottleneck? Consider that if, over two thousand years ago, they (4) ...... **(employ)** a scribe to distribute royal decrees across the empire in a single day, he would have needed a far lighter writing material than clay or stone tablets.

Many ancient civilisations had no equivalent of paper, so there would have been no efficient means of mass written communication. Of course, the truth is that if these hypothetical societies (5) ...... **(have)** access to paper, it would be impossible for them to have had it without first mastering the art of pulp-making.
If the technique of paper-making (6) ...... **(develop)**, we would not have the publishing industry, the education system, or the written exam as we know it.

Modern education depends absolutely on the widespread availability of affordable written materials. It may seem obvious to us now that paper should exist, but early philosophers found its very concept troubling. Medieval scholars debated endlessly: 'If nothing solid (7) ...... **(be)** reducible to fibres, then paper itself would be a kind of impossibility.'
'And if it (8) ...... **(do)** turn out to be possible, then the entire notion of impermanence in writing would be overturned.'
If it (9) ...... **(be)** for a Chinese court official named Cai Lun, who refined the papermaking process around 105 AD, who knows how differently civilisation might have evolved?

He produced the earliest recorded account of true papermaking using bark, hemp, and rags. If he (10) ...... **(do)**, the book — and everything that followed from it — might never have come into existence.

sections.title: B: Hypothetical Situations
sections.instructions: Write the verbs in brackets in the correct form in each gap to make hypothetical situations.

items.sentence: 1. Supposing you ...... (have) the opportunity to spend a year studying at a university abroad, would you take it?
2. What if we ...... (decide) to give up our jobs and travel the world for a year?
3. Suppose all your colleagues ...... (say) they found you difficult to work with and you found out. How would you respond?
4. Imagine you ...... (have) access to a phone, computer, or any digital device for an entire month. How would you manage?
5. What if the printing press ...... (invent)? How might history have unfolded differently?
6. Supposing all public transport ...... (stop) operating overnight, how would people get to work the next morning?
7. Imagine we ...... (just / tell) that a severe storm was heading directly towards the city. What steps would be most important to take?
8. Suppose you ...... (can) speak any language in the world fluently without studying it. Which would you choose?
9. What if every country in the world ...... (speak) a different invented language with no shared tongue?
10. Suppose it ...... (prove) that certain plants were capable of experiencing pain.
11. I know it's almost certainly not going to happen, but imagine this small café ...... (become) the most celebrated restaurant in the country!
12. What if you ...... (give) a private island to develop entirely as you wished? What would you build first?
13. Supposing you ...... (wake) up one morning to find yourself in a completely unfamiliar country, how would you go about finding your way?
14. I know it's physically impossible, but suppose for a moment that water ...... (be) able to flow naturally upward.
15. Imagine that humans ...... (can) use written language. How do you think civilisation would have developed?

sections.title: C: As if / As though (Comprehension)
sections.instructions: Look at the questions that follow each sentence and circle the answer, A, B or C which is not correct.

items.sentence: 1. Why does Elena always behave as if she were the only competent person in the whole department?
2. Patrick often talks as if he had grown up in an extremely privileged household.
3. The hikers look as if they've been walking uphill for hours.
4. Don't worry about bringing it up. Just speak to her as if you didn't know she'd been through such a difficult period.
5. It was really unsettling. At one point, it felt like we weren't ever going to find our way back through the fog.
6. Omar looked as if he had just received some unexpectedly wonderful news.

sections.title: D: As if / As though (Verb Forms)
sections.instructions: Write the verbs in brackets in the correct form in each gap. When a situation is hypothetical or unlikely, use unreal tenses.

items.sentence: 1 Nadia speaks as though she ……………………. (grow up) in Paris, yet she only moved there six months ago.
2 By the time Carlos arrived at the venue, he looked as though he ………………………… (drag) through a muddy field.
3 The pain in my knee was so severe that I honestly felt as if I ………………………… (go) to collapse right there on the pitch.
4 When I saw Priya at the conference last Thursday, it felt as if she ………………………… (give) keynote speeches for years. I couldn't believe it was actually her first one!
5 Theo looked as if he ………………………… (receive) the worst possible news. In fact, he'd just been told the project was cancelled.
6 Don't you think Sophie sometimes looks as though she ………………………… (wish) she had pursued a completely different career path?
7 Marcus always dresses as though he ………………………… (roll) in money, yet everyone knows he's barely making ends meet.
8 You two are acting as if tomorrow ………………………… (be) a public holiday. I assure you, it isn't!
9 How can you just sit there so calmly as if nothing ………………………… (happen)?

sections.title: E: Polite Questions and Requests
sections.instructions: Make each of these statements or questions as polite as possible by using past tenses.

items.sentence: 1 What size ...... (you, want) to try on, sir?
2 I ...... (look) for a suitable anniversary present for my parents.
3 We ...... (wonder) whether you ...... (want) to join us for dinner on Friday evening.
4 Excuse me. ...... (you, plan) to leave your luggage here for long?
5 ...... (you, wish) to make a reservation, or just have a look at the menu?
6 ...... (you, look) for something in a specific price range?
7 ...... (you, want) still or sparkling water with your meal?
8 I ...... (hope) you ...... (give) us a little more time to think it over.
9 How long ...... (you, intend) to keep the vehicle?
10 I ...... (wonder) if you ...... (pay) by card or in cash, madam.

sections.title: F: It's (high/about) time
sections.instructions: Write a verb from the box in the correct form in each gap.

items.sentence: 1. It's high time you ………………… complaining and did something constructive about it.
2. It's time you ………………… into renting your own flat. You're nearly thirty!
3. I really think it's time we ………………… a new washing machine. This one has been breaking down constantly.
4. It's time for the committee ………………… something about the car park situation.
5. It's about time the local council ………………… investing in the park again.
6. Come on, Leo. It's time ………………… or you'll miss your train.
7. Don't you think it's time you ………………… a promotion after all these years?
8. It's time for her ………………… team leader — she's more than earned it.
9. It's definitely time I ………………… to make my own decisions around here.
10. We really should make a move. It's time for us ………………… before the traffic gets bad.
11. Isn't it about time he ………………… to manage his own finances properly?
12. Is this a good moment ………………… the clinic, or would it be better to wait until this afternoon?

sections.title: G: Would rather / sooner
sections.instructions: Write a word or short phrase in each gap so that each second sentence has a similar meaning to the first sentence.

items.sentence: 1 The director would prefer the new intern to have some prior experience in the field. The director would rather the new intern ……………………………………… some experience in the field.
2 Would you prefer me to return after lunch? Would you rather …………………………………… after lunch?
3 Quite frankly, I'd prefer you not to mention this to anyone else. Quite frankly, I'd rather …………………………………… anyone else about this.
4 I'd prefer us to get a new boiler rather than go on a costly city break this winter. I'd sooner ………………………………………… a new boiler than ………………………………… on a costly city break this winter.
5 Mum said she'd prefer Lucas to be living closer to home, and I agree entirely. Mum said she'd rather Lucas …………………………………… closer to home, and I agree entirely.
6 I would prefer her to be enrolled on a training programme rather than made redundant. I would rather ………………………………………… on a training programme than made redundant.
7 Honestly, I'd prefer us not to bring this up in front of the children, if that's alright. Honestly, I'd sooner ………………………………………… about this in front of the children, if that's alright.
8 Of course I would have preferred you to be upfront with me from the very beginning! Of course I would rather ………………………………… me the truth from the beginning!
9 I believe Elisa would definitely prefer to have a small, intimate ceremony. I believe Elisa would definitely rather ………………………………… a small, intimate ceremony.
10 We would have preferred them not to have overstayed their welcome, if we're being honest. We would sooner …………………………………… their welcome, if we're being honest.

sections.title: H: Wish / If only
sections.instructions: Circle the correct word or phrase. If both are correct, circle both.

items.sentence: 1. All my classmates have laptops. I wish I **have / had** one too!
2. Don't you sometimes wish you **ran / were running** your own business?
3. If only we **didn't leave / hadn't left** our umbrellas at home!
4. If only we **don't / didn't** have to work this weekend!
5. I do wish the plumber **came / would come**. The leak is getting worse.
6. Do you ever wish that you **sang / could sing** opera really well?
7. There is one thing I truly regret. I wish I could **go / have gone** to see that concert, but at the time it just wasn't possible.
8. Excuse me, I wish **spoke / to speak** to your manager, please.
9. I **wish / hope** the weather holds for the weekend!
10. Why on earth does Marcus wish he **would be promoted / they would give him** a raise already?
11. It's probably not the wisest investment, but I do wish you **luck / lucky** with it.
12. My grandmother always wished that she was **/ had been** born in a different era.
13. If only **you were / you'd been** paying attention in class instead of texting. You wouldn't have missed that explanation.
14. I do wish this meeting **didn't go / wasn't going** on quite so long. We all have other things to do!
15. If only Jamie **wasn't / hadn't been** injured. I'm certain the team would have won the match.
1. Imagine ………………… when the first steam engines were being built. How would your daily life have been different?
2. What if your neighbour's dog suddenly ………………… to you in perfect English right now? How would you react?
3. Would Edison have become so famous if he ………………… the lightbulb?
4. It's high time someone ………………… something about the broken heating in this building.
5. It's remarkable how Kate behaves as though she and her flatmates ………………… any tension between them at all.
6. ………………… both of you like to take a seat while you wait?
7. If you ………………… to cancel your reservation, would you still be charged a fee?
8. Apparently, Hannah would rather we ………………… too much fuss about her leaving do next week.

sections.title: J: Key Word Transformation
sections.instructions: Complete the second sentence so that it has a similar meaning to the first sentence, using the word given. Do not change the word given. You must use between three and eight words, including the word given.

items.sentence: 1 Oliver doesn't want to be assigned the lead role in the presentation. rather Oliver a lead role in the presentation.
2 What a shame we didn't manage to get tickets for the concert. only If to get tickets for the concert!
3 Why don't the neighbours stop parking in front of our gate? wish I parking in front of our gate!
4 What a shame I didn't get to see her reaction when she won the award. could I wish her reaction when she won the award.
5 Don't you think the company should bring out an updated version of the software? about Don't you think it's out an updated version of the software?
6 I only found out about the cancellation because Miriam rang me. if I wouldn't have known about the cancellation me.
